name: Netherlands

outbound_official.note: No official Netherlands government outbound visa-list page located. The Dutch Ministry of Foreign Affairs (nederlandwereldwijd.nl / rijksoverheid.nl) does not publish a centralized list of visa-free/visa-on-arrival/e-visa/ETA destinations for Netherlands ordinary-passport holders. Instead, citizens are directed to check per-country travel advisories at https://www.nederlandwereldwijd.nl/reisadvies and look under 'Paspoort, visum, rijbewijs' for each destination. The only partial official list found is https://www.rijksoverheid.nl/onderwerpen/paspoort-en-identiteitskaart/vraag-en-antwoord/naar-welke-landen-reizen-met-nederlandse-identiteitskaart (42 countries where the Dutch ID card is accepted), which is not a comprehensive outbound visa policy list. Rely on destination-side data.

visa_free.nationality: EU/EEA member state nationals (all 27 EU member states + Iceland, Norway, Liechtenstein)
visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens have the right to free movement and may reside in the Netherlands without restriction. No short-stay cap applies under freedom of movement rules.
Swiss nationals have free movement rights equivalent to EEA citizens under the EU-Switzerland bilateral agreements.

visa_free.nationality: Hong Kong SAR (holders of 'Hong Kong Special Administrative Region' passport)
visa_free.notes: Visa-free for stays of no more than 90 days in any 180-day period. Applies only to holders of a 'Hong Kong Special Administrative Region' passport. Source: EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex II, Section 2 (consolidated as of 03 February 2025).

visa_free.nationality: Macao SAR (holders of 'Região Administrativa Especial de Macau' passport)
visa_free.notes: Visa-free for stays of no more than 90 days in any 180-day period. Applies only to holders of a 'Região Administrativa Especial de Macau' passport. Source: EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex II, Section 2 (consolidated as of 03 February 2025).

visa_free.nationality: British nationals (Overseas) / British overseas territories citizens / British overseas citizens / British protected persons / British subjects (non-UK nationals)
visa_free.notes: Visa-free for stays of no more than 90 days in any 180-day period. These are British citizens who are not nationals of the United Kingdom for purposes of Union law. Source: EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex II, Section 3 (consolidated as of 03 February 2025).

UK passport holders are visa-free for the Netherlands under the Schengen short-stay rules (post-Brexit). UK was added to Annex II by amendment. Max 90 days in any 180-day period. Source: EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex II (consolidated as of 03 February 2025).

visa_free.nationality: Taiwan (holders of passports issued by Taiwan with an identity card number)
visa_free.notes: Visa-free for stays of no more than 90 days in any 180-day period. Applies only to holders of passports issued by Taiwan which include an identity card number. Source: EU Regulation 2018/1806 Annex II, Section 4 (consolidated as of 03 February 2025).

eta.notes: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) will be required for all visa-exempt travelers visiting the Netherlands and other Schengen area countries. Expected to start operations in the last quarter of 2026 (Q4 2026). Authorization valid for up to 90 days per 180-day period. As of June 2026, ETIAS is not yet operational; no action required from travellers at this time.

visa_policy.notes: Netherlands is a member of the Schengen Area and EU. Visa policy for short stays (up to 90 days in any 180-day period) is governed by EU Regulation 2018/1806 (as amended and consolidated to 30 December 2025). Countries in Annex II are visa-free; countries in Annex I require a Schengen visa. Notable 2023–2025 amendments: Kosovo added to Annex II effective 1 January 2024 (Reg. 2023/850); Vanuatu transferred from Annex II to Annex I effective 3 February 2025 (Reg. 2025/11 of 19 December 2024). Regulation (EU) 2025/2441 (10 December 2025, effective 30 December 2025) introduced a reinforced visa suspension mechanism (new Articles 8a–8f) but made no changes to Annex I or Annex II country lists. As of 6 March 2026, Georgia's visa-free access for diplomatic/service/official passport holders has been suspended until 6 March 2027 under the new suspension mechanism; ordinary biometric passport holders from Georgia remain visa-free. The Netherlands does not have a national visa-on-arrival or e-visa system for short stays; Schengen visa applications are processed at Dutch embassies. ETIAS is expected to apply to visa-free travelers in Q4 2026. The EES (Entry/Exit System) began operations October 12, 2025.

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of Chinese diplomatic passports are visa-free for short stays up to 90 days in any 180-day period in the Schengen Area (including European Netherlands). Based on EU-China Agreement on short-stay visa waiver for holders of diplomatic passports, Council Decision (EU) 2016/431, in force 1 January 2017. Applies to all Schengen member states. Ordinary Chinese passports still require a Schengen visa.

Holders of Brazilian diplomatic, service, or official passports are visa-free for short stays up to 90 days in any 180-day period in the Schengen Area (including European Netherlands). Based on EU-Brazil Agreement on short-stay visa waiver for holders of diplomatic, service or official passports, in force 10 April 2011 (amended 2022). Separate from the ordinary passport agreement (in force 1 October 2012). Applies to Schengen member states.

credential.subtype: valid regular residence permit (non-asylum) of a different Schengen country

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid regular (non-asylum) residence permit issued by another Schengen country are exempt from the MVV (Machtiging tot Voorlopig Verblijf / authorisation for temporary stay) requirement to enter the Netherlands for long-stay purposes, provided they have a recognised sponsor in the Netherlands and meet all other requirements. This is an MVV-waiver for long-stay entry, not a Schengen short-stay provision. Source: IND MVV Exemptions page.

credential.subtype: long-term EU resident permit issued by another EU country

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a long-term EU resident permit (issued by another EU member state) are exempt from the MVV requirement to enter the Netherlands for long-stay purposes. Spouse/partner and minor children who lived together in that other EU country are also exempt. This is an MVV-waiver for long-stay entry. Source: IND MVV Exemptions page.

credential.subtype: European Blue Card held in another participating EU/EEA country for at least 12 months

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid European Blue Card issued by another EU/EEA country (held for at least 12 months) who are applying for a Blue Card in the Netherlands are exempt from the MVV requirement. Spouse/partner and minor children are also exempt. This is an MVV-waiver for long-stay entry. Source: IND MVV Exemptions page.

credential.subtype: residence permit as researcher under Directive (EU) 2016/801 issued by another EU country

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a researcher permit (issued under Directive 2016/801) in another EU country applying for long-term mobility researcher status in the Netherlands are exempt from the MVV requirement. Spouse/partner and minor children are also exempt. This is an MVV-waiver for long-stay entry. Source: IND MVV Exemptions page.

credential.subtype: residence permit as intra-corporate transferee issued by another EU country for more than 90 days

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a residence permit as intra-corporate transferee issued by another EU country (for a stay of more than 90 days) who are being transferred to a Netherlands branch are exempt from the MVV requirement. Spouse/partner and minor children are also exempt. This is an MVV-waiver for long-stay entry. Source: IND MVV Exemptions page.

credential.subtype: multiple-entry visa (not an ESTA) for the United States

conditional_access.conditions: Nationals of Bolivia, China, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti, India, and Venezuela holding a valid multiple-entry US visa may enter all Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. Jamaica nationals with a valid multiple-entry US visa may enter all Caribbean parts EXCEPT Curaçao (they are already visa-free for Curaçao by nationality). Guyana nationals with a US multiple-entry visa may enter Sint Maarten (they otherwise require a visa only for Sint Maarten). NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: multiple-entry visa (not an ESTA equivalent) for Canada

conditional_access.conditions: Nationals of Bolivia, China, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti, India, and Venezuela holding a valid multiple-entry Canadian visa may enter all Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. Jamaica nationals with a valid multiple-entry Canadian visa may enter all Caribbean parts EXCEPT Curaçao (they are already visa-free for Curaçao by nationality). Guyana nationals with a Canadian multiple-entry visa may enter Sint Maarten. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: residence permit for the United States

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid US residence permit (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

Holders of a valid Canadian residence permit (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: multiple-entry visa for the United Kingdom

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid multiple-entry UK visa (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: residence permit for the United Kingdom

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid UK residence permit (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which is Schengen). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: multiple-entry short-stay Schengen visa issued by any Schengen country

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid multiple-entry short-stay Schengen visa (any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch Caribbean visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which has its own Schengen short-stay rules). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: residence permit for the Netherlands or another Schengen country

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid Schengen residence permit (Netherlands or another Schengen country) of any nationality may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch Caribbean visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands (which has its own Schengen short-stay rules). Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

credential.subtype: valid residence permit for French Guiana, Guadeloupe, Martinique, Mayotte, Réunion, Saint Barthélemy, or Saint Martin

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid residence permit for a French overseas territory (French Guiana, Guadeloupe, Martinique, Mayotte, Réunion, Saint Barthélemy, or Saint Martin) of any nationality may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch Caribbean visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands. Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

Holders of a valid multiple-entry Irish visa (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch Caribbean visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands. Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

Holders of a valid Irish residence permit (of any nationality) may enter the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius, Saba) without a separate Dutch Caribbean visa for up to 90 days. NOTE: This rule applies ONLY to the Caribbean parts of the Kingdom - NOT to European Netherlands. Source: NetherlandsWorldwide official government website.

cbi.notes: The Netherlands does not have a citizenship-by-investment program. The Admission Scheme for Foreign Investors (residence permit requiring €1.25 million investment in an innovative Dutch business) was abolished on April 17, 2024. New applications are no longer accepted. Existing permit holders may still extend under pre-abolition rules. The standard route to Dutch citizenship is through naturalization after 5 years of legal residence.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Self-Employed Person (Zelfstandige)
programs.notes: For foreign nationals who wish to work in the Netherlands as self-employed or entrepreneur. Applicant's work must be of 'essential interest to the Dutch economy.' Assessed by Netherlands Enterprise Agency (RVO) using a points system (minimum 30 points per category: personal experience, business plan, added value). No minimum investment amount specified for the general route. Alternative routes exist under the Dutch-American Friendship Treaty or Dutch-Japanese Trade Treaty (requiring 'substantial capital' - typically at least €4,500). Permit valid up to 2 years; renewable. Path to permanent residence after 5 years.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Essential Start-Up Personnel
programs.notes: Pilot program running from 1 June 2021 to 1 June 2028. For foreign employees essential to an innovative, scalable start-up in the Netherlands. The start-up must have max 15 employees and can sponsor up to 5 foreign employees. Employee must be an expert in a specialized field, and must receive mandatory participation: at least 1% equity stake OR financial value of €65,000+ through shares/options. RVO provides advisory assessment. Permit valid up to 3 years. Path to permanent residence after 5 years.

programs.name: Highly Skilled Migrant (Kennismigrant)
programs.eligibility: Must have employment contract with an IND-recognised sponsor; salary must meet minimum thresholds (gross per month, valid from 1 January 2026): reduced criterion €3,122/month (for recent graduates/researchers, within 3 years of graduation/permit expiry); under 30 years old €4,357/month; 30 years or older €5,942/month. Healthcare professionals must be BIG-registered if in listed professions.
programs.notes: Salary thresholds (gross per month, valid from 1 January 2026 - thresholds update annually each 1 January): reduced criterion €3,122; under-30 €4,357; 30+ years €5,942. Only IND-recognised employers may apply. Permit valid up to 5 years. Application fee €423. Path to permanent residence after 5 years. Source: ind.nl/en/required-amounts-income-requirements.

programs.name: European Blue Card
programs.eligibility: Higher education diploma (minimum 3 years) relevant to role, OR 5 years of relevant professional experience at higher education level (3 years for IT professionals within 7 years). Employment contract minimum 6 months with Dutch-based employer. Salary thresholds (gross per month, valid from 1 January 2026): standard €5,942/month; recent graduates (within 3 years) €4,754/month.
programs.processing_time: 30 days (recognised sponsors / long-term mobility); 45 days (certain existing permit holders); 90 days (all others)
programs.notes: Salary thresholds (gross per month, valid from 1 January 2026 - thresholds update annually each 1 January): standard €5,942; reduced for recent graduates €4,754. Foreign diplomas evaluated by Nuffic. Path to permanent residence may be accelerated (under 5 years) for Blue Card holders. Permit valid up to 5 years. Application fee €423. Source: ind.nl/en/required-amounts-income-requirements.

programs.name: Orientation Year for Highly Educated Migrants (Zoekjaar)
programs.eligibility: Must have completed an accredited bachelor's/master's/post-master's degree at a Dutch institution, OR an Erasmus Mundus Joint Master, OR a master's/doctoral from a top-200 ranked institution (Times Higher Education, QS, Shanghai), within 3 years before application. Also available to recent research permit holders. Must not have previously held an orientation year permit.
programs.notes: Permits free work during the 1-year validity. Permit is not extendable. Application fee €254 (exempt for Turkish, San Marinese, and Israeli nationals). Foreign diplomas evaluated by Nuffic. Designed to allow graduates and researchers to find employment in the Netherlands as a stepping stone to a highly skilled migrant or Blue Card permit.

programs.name: National Highly Skilled Migrant Scheme
programs.eligibility: For highly skilled migrants where the European Blue Card does not apply; based on national Dutch immigration rules rather than EU directive.
programs.notes: Parallel to the EU Blue Card scheme, the Netherlands operates its own national highly skilled migrant scheme. Salary thresholds and requirements similar to those of the Highly Skilled Migrant permit. Designed to complement the EU Blue Card framework.

programs.name: Working Holiday Residence Permit
programs.eligibility: Available to nationals of Japan (max 200/year), South Korea (max 200/year), Argentina (max 100/year), Uruguay (max 100/year), Hong Kong (max 100/year), Taiwan (max 100/year), Australia (no fixed quota), Canada (no fixed quota), New Zealand (no fixed quota). Must be aged 18–30 at time of application. Must have return ticket or funds for return ticket and sufficient funds for the stay.
programs.processing_time: Approximately 2–3 weeks after arrival for permit card
programs.notes: Annual quota applies for Japan, South Korea, Argentina, Uruguay, Hong Kong, and Taiwan. Australia, Canada, and New Zealand nationals have no fixed quota and do not need an MVV - they apply upon arrival. As of April 2026, Japan quota for 2026 was exhausted (7 April 2026); South Korea quota still available. From 1 April 2026, pre-registration no longer required - applicants must apply for permit directly. Allows free work in Netherlands during stay. Not extendable.

agreements: EU freedom of movement (EU citizens may reside and work freely in Netherlands)
EEA freedom of movement (Iceland, Norway, Liechtenstein nationals equivalent to EU citizens)
Schengen Area (Netherlands is a founding Schengen member; no internal border controls with other Schengen states)
EU-Switzerland bilateral agreement on free movement of persons

validation_notes: CORRECTION 1 (critical): Vanuatu (VUT) REMOVED from visa_free list. Regulation (EU) 2025/11 of 19 December 2024 transferred Vanuatu from Annex II to Annex I, restoring the visa requirement effective 3 February 2025. The prior record incorrectly classified Vanuatu as visa-free. Source: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2024/12/12/vanuatu-council-ends-visa-exemption/

CORRECTION 2 (critical): Kosovo (XKX) ADDED to visa_free list. Regulation (EU) 2023/850 added Kosovo to Annex II effective 1 January 2024 for holders of biometric passports. Kosovo was entirely absent from the prior record.

CORRECTION 3: Caribbean conditional_access entries for US and Canadian visa (Jamaica / Guyana island-specific exceptions) - clarified wording. The prior record stated Jamaica holders of US/Canadian visa can enter 'all Caribbean parts except Curaçao,' which was ambiguous. Corrected to: Jamaica nationals are visa-free for Curaçao by nationality alone; with a US/Canadian multiple-entry visa they can also enter the other Caribbean islands. Guyana nationals require a visa only for Sint Maarten; with a US/Canadian multiple-entry visa they can enter Sint Maarten.

CORRECTION 4: Working Holiday permit - Australia, Canada, and New Zealand added as eligible nationalities (they had no fixed annual quota and were omitted from the prior record). Confirmed by IND official page and secondary sources.

CORRECTION 5: Salary threshold validity dates - prior record stated 'valid 1 January 2026 – 30 June 2026'; corrected to 'valid from 1 January 2026' (IND page states thresholds change annually on 1 January, not semi-annually). The '30 June' cut-off was not found on the current official IND page.
CORRECTION 6: Source URLs for Annex II entries updated from the original 2018 PDF to the consolidated 2025 version (CELEX:02018R1806-20250203).
CORRECTION 7: ETIAS notes updated - official government.nl page now states launch is expected 'in the last quarter of 2026' (Q4 2026), not generically 'late 2026'.
UNCHANGED: cbi, rbi programs, diplomatic passport conditional_access, MVV-waiver credential entries, EEA/CH free movement - all verified as still current.

RE-VERIFICATION 2026-06-27: Re-fetched EU Regulation 2018/1806 consolidated versions (02/2025 and 12/2025), government.nl/ETIAS, ind.nl/working-holiday, home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/visa-policy. No changes to Annex II country list found for ordinary passport holders between February 2025 and June 2026. Regulation (EU) 2025/2441 (30 December 2025) introduced enhanced visa suspension mechanism (Articles 8a-8f) only - no Annex I/II country-list changes. Georgia suspension effective 6 March 2026 applies ONLY to diplomatic/service/official passports (already in conditional_access); Georgia ordinary biometric passport holders remain in visa_free list - no change required. ETIAS Q4 2026 timeline reconfirmed. Working holiday IND page reconfirmed: Japan 2026 quota exhausted from 7 April 2026; South Korea quota still open; Australia/Canada/New Zealand no fixed quota (all confirmed). Main notes field updated to reference the December 2025 consolidation and note Reg 2025/2441 and the Georgia suspension.

RE-VERIFICATION 2026-06-29: Re-fetched government.nl/ETIAS (Q4 2026 timeline confirmed unchanged), ind.nl/working-holiday (confirmed 9 nationalities, quota status unchanged), ind.nl/required-amounts-income-requirements (HSM and Blue Card salary thresholds from 1 Jan 2026 confirmed), ind.nl/mvv-exemptions (last updated 13 May 2026, content unchanged), ind.nl/fees (au pair fee corrected to €423 - see CORRECTION 8). WebSearch and home-affairs.ec.europa.eu confirmed no new countries added to or removed from Annex II in 2026. No new visa waiver agreements entered into force between February 2025 and June 2026. Georgia ordinary passport holders remain visa-free. All conditional_access entries verified unchanged. CORRECTION 8: Au Pair Residence Permit fee corrected from €254 to €423 per live IND fees page (ind.nl/en/fees confirmed €423 for first application). This is a visa_types fee correction, not a visa_policy or access-level change.
gaps: The consolidated EU Regulation 2018/1806 (CELEX:02018R1806-20250203 and 20251230) was accessed via WebFetch but some section-level footnotes (e.g., exact status of individual bilateral agreements for Dominica, Micronesia, Grenada, Kiribati, Marshall Islands, Nauru, Palau, Peru, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Timor-Leste, Tonga, Tuvalu, Antigua and Barbuda, UAE marked as 'from date of entry into force') could not be verified as having entered into force by June 2026. These remain as conditionally visa-free per Annex II text.

Netherlands national bilateral visa waiver agreements for diplomatic/service passports beyond the EU-level agreements (China diplomatic, Brazil diplomatic/service/official) remain unverified from a comprehensive Dutch MFA list. A full country-by-country list of diplomatic passport exemptions was not accessible from official sources.
No official Dutch source was found granting any special entry benefit to OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholders for entry to the Netherlands.

The Caribbean conditional access rules note specific island-level exceptions for Jamaica (Curaçao visa-free, others require visa) and Guyana (Sint Maarten requires visa, others do not). Full island-by-island enumeration for every nationality under every credential type was not completed.
Haitian nationals transiting through Curaçao airport require a transit visa - this airport transit visa requirement was not separately modelled in the conditional_access array.
Working holiday quota availability for Australia, Canada, and New Zealand was not confirmed with a specific annual number from the official IND page; these are described as having no fixed quota.

Georgia suspension mechanism (Reg. 2025/2441) could potentially escalate to cover ordinary passport holders if the suspension is extended to a second stage. No official announcement of escalation as of June 2026; Georgia ordinary passport holders remain visa-free. Monitor EUR-Lex for further amendments.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C)
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals from visa-required countries to visit the Netherlands for tourism, family visits, or short business trips for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €80 for adults, €40 for children 6–12, free for children under 6. Applied for at the Dutch embassy or consulate. For nationals of countries in Annex I of EU Regulation 2018/1806. The Netherlands does not offer a national e-visa or visa-on-arrival for short stays. Extensions cost €30. Applications must be submitted at a Dutch embassy, consulate, or authorized visa application centre.

visa_types.name: Airport Transit Visa (Type A)
visa_types.purpose: Required for certain nationalities transiting through an international zone at a Dutch airport (e.g., Amsterdam Schiphol) without entering the Schengen area.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €80 for adults, €40 for children 6–12, free for children under 6. Required for nationals of specific high-risk countries when transiting through Schengen international zones. Separate from the Type C short-stay visa. Applied for at the Dutch embassy or consulate.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C) – Business
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals from visa-required countries to visit the Netherlands for business meetings, conferences, or short-term commercial activities for up to 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Same visa category as tourist (Type C Schengen visa). Purpose declared at application. Applied for at Dutch embassy or consulate. Visa-free nationals (Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806) do not need a visa for short business stays.

visa_types.name: Highly Skilled Migrant Residence Permit (Kennismigrant)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay residence permit for highly skilled foreign nationals employed by an IND-recognised sponsor in the Netherlands, allowing them to live and work in the country.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Permit validity matches contract duration, max 5 years. Requires employment with IND-recognised sponsor. Salary thresholds (gross/month, from 1 Jan 2026): reduced criterion €3,122; under 30 years €4,357; 30+ years €5,942. If job is lost, 3–6 months to find new employment. Path to permanent residence after 5 years.

visa_types.name: European Blue Card Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: EU-level work permit for highly qualified non-EU nationals with a university degree or equivalent experience, employed in a high-skilled role in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Requires higher education diploma (min 3 years) or 5 years professional experience at higher level. Salary thresholds (gross/month, from 1 Jan 2026): standard €5,942; recent graduates (within 3 years) €4,754. Contract minimum 6 months. Processing: 30 days for recognised sponsors, 45 days for certain existing holders, 90 days for others. Valid up to 5 years.

visa_types.name: Single Permit (GVVA – Gecombineerde Vergunning voor Verblijf en Arbeid)
visa_types.purpose: Combined residence and work permit for non-EU nationals taking up paid employment in the Netherlands that does not fall under the highly skilled migrant or Blue Card schemes.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Valid up to 3 years (or 5 years if applicant has worked legally for 5 years in the Netherlands). Employer must apply on behalf of the employee. Employer must be registered in the Dutch Chamber of Commerce, advertise the vacancy through UWV, and pay salary per applicable collective labour agreement. Covers various roles including spiritual counselors, artists, intra-corporate transferees, and international non-profit employees.

visa_types.name: Intra-Corporate Transferee Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Residence permit for managers, specialists, or trainees being transferred from a non-EU company to a branch or subsidiary in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Governed by EU Directive 2014/66/EU. Transferee must have worked for the company group for at least 3 months. Valid for managers/specialists up to 3 years; trainees up to 1 year. Eligible for EU intra-corporate transferee mobility to other EU member states.

visa_types.name: Self-Employed Person Residence Permit (Zelfstandige)
visa_types.purpose: Residence permit for non-EU nationals who wish to establish and run their own business or work as a freelancer in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Applicant's work must be of 'essential interest to the Dutch economy,' assessed by RVO (Netherlands Enterprise Agency) via a points system (minimum 30 points per category). No minimum investment amount for general route; Dutch-American and Dutch-Japanese trade treaties require 'substantial capital' (typically at least €4,500). Permit valid up to 2 years; renewable. Path to permanent residence after 5 years.

visa_types.name: Start-Up Founder Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Temporary residence permit for non-EU entrepreneurs who want to develop an innovative business in the Netherlands with the guidance of an approved facilitator.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Valid for 1 year, not extendable. Applicant must work with an approved facilitator under a signed contract. Business must be innovative (new to the Netherlands or using new technology). After 1 year, can apply for self-employed person permit. RVO provides advisory assessment and positive assessment is required. Requires registration in Chamber of Commerce Trade Register.

visa_types.name: Seasonal Work Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Temporary work permit for non-EU nationals performing seasonal agricultural or horticultural work in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254. Maximum stay 24 weeks (168 days). Primarily for agricultural sector. Employer must apply on behalf of the worker. Worker must be paid at least minimum wage.

visa_types.name: Researcher Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Residence permit for non-EU nationals conducting scientific research in the Netherlands under a hosting agreement with a recognised research institution.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423. Governed by EU Directive 2016/801. Eligible for EU long-term mobility to other member states after at least 6 months in the Netherlands. Recognised research organisations must host the researcher under a formal hosting agreement.

visa_types.name: Student Residence Permit (University / Higher Professional Education)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay residence permit for non-EU/EEA nationals enrolled in an accredited bachelor's, master's, or doctoral programme at a Dutch university or higher professional education institution.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254 for first application and extensions. Typically applied for by the educational institution on the student's behalf. Valid for 1 year; renewable for duration of study. Institution must be IND-recognised. Work is permitted up to 16 hours/week or full-time during summer months.

visa_types.name: Student Residence Permit (Secondary / Vocational Education)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay residence permit for non-EU/EEA nationals enrolled in secondary school (VMBO/HAVO/VWO) or vocational education (MBO) in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254. For students studying below university level. Educational institution typically applies on behalf of the student. Renewable for duration of study.

visa_types.name: Orientation Year Residence Permit (Zoekjaar – Highly Educated Migrants)
visa_types.purpose: One-year residence permit for recent graduates or researchers who completed their studies/research in the Netherlands or at a top-ranked institution abroad, allowing them to seek employment or start a business.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254 (exempt for Turkish, San Marinese, and Israeli nationals). Valid for 1 year; not extendable. Must have completed accredited bachelor's/master's/doctoral degree at Dutch institution, or Erasmus Mundus Joint Master, or master's/doctoral from top-200 ranked institution (THE, QS, Shanghai) within 3 years before application. Permits free work during validity. Not available if orientation year permit was previously held.

visa_types.name: Working Holiday Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Residence permit for young nationals of specific countries allowing them to live and work in the Netherlands on a temporary basis, primarily to experience Dutch culture while financing their stay through employment.
visa_types.notes: Available to nationals of: Australia (no quota), Canada (no quota), New Zealand (no quota), Japan (200/year), South Korea (200/year), Argentina (100/year), Uruguay (100/year), Hong Kong (100/year), Taiwan (100/year). Applicants must be aged 18–30. Must have return ticket or funds to purchase one, and sufficient funds for the stay. Australia, Canada, and New Zealand nationals do not need an MVV; they apply upon arrival. Quota countries must apply before quota is exhausted. Not extendable. From 1 April 2026, pre-registration is no longer required.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Residence Permit (Partner)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay residence permit allowing the foreign partner or spouse of a Dutch or lawfully resident non-EU national to live in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254 for adults; €85 for children under 18; €44–€85 for Turkish nationals (reduced rates under EU-Turkey Association Agreement). Sponsor must meet income requirement (generally 100% of applicable social assistance norm). Sponsor must have a valid residence permit of at least 1 year. Relationship must be genuine. Permit valid 1 year; renewable.

visa_types.name: Family Reunification Residence Permit (Minor Child)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay residence permit for non-EU minor children (under 18) to join a parent lawfully residing in the Netherlands.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €85 for children under 18. Parent sponsor must meet income requirements and have a valid residence permit. Permit valid 1 year; renewable.

visa_types.name: Medical Treatment Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Residence permit for non-EU nationals who need to receive medical treatment in the Netherlands that is not available in their home country.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €1,337 for first application; €466 for extension. High fee reflects the specialised nature of this permit. Treatment must be unavailable in applicant's country of origin. Applicant must demonstrate sufficient financial means to cover treatment and living expenses. Permit tied to the duration of treatment.

visa_types.name: Return Visa (Terugkeervisum)
visa_types.purpose: A national visa for non-EU nationals who hold a Dutch residence permit that has expired or is being renewed, allowing them to return to the Netherlands after travelling abroad.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €197 standard; €57 for EC-Turkey Agreement holders; free for Moluccans. Issued by the Dutch embassy or consulate abroad when a permit holder has left the Netherlands and their permit has been renewed or is pending. Allows re-entry to the Netherlands.

visa_types.name: Au Pair Residence Permit
visa_types.purpose: Temporary residence permit for young foreign nationals to live with a Dutch host family, providing childcare and light household tasks in exchange for board and pocket money, as part of a cultural exchange.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €423 (first application and change in purpose). Host family must be approved; au pair must be aged 18–30 (some exceptions). Duration typically 1 year. Applicant must have adequate health insurance. Not a work permit; income is limited to pocket money. Cultural exchange focus. Note: fee updated from €254 to €423 as of 2026 IND fee schedule (confirmed from ind.nl/en/fees).

visa_types.name: Long-Term Resident EU Status (Duurzame Verblijfsvergunning EU)
visa_types.purpose: Permanent long-term resident status within the EU for non-EU nationals who have legally resided in the Netherlands for at least 5 continuous years.
visa_types.notes: Fee is €254 for first application; €85 for 5-year renewal. Requires 5 years legal continuous residence. Provides near-equal rights to Dutch nationals and facilitates mobility to other EU member states. Renewable every 5 years for €85. Governed by EU Directive 2003/109/EC.
